source_paper_bibtex: @article{Caragiannis_2025, title={On Interim Envy-Free Allocation Lotteries}, volume={50}, ISSN={1526-5471}, url={http://dx.doi.org/10.1287/moor.2023.0203}, DOI={10.1287/moor.2023.0203}, number={4}, journal={Mathematics of Operations Research}, publisher={Institute for Operations Research and the Management Sciences (INFORMS)}, author={Caragiannis, Ioannis and Kanellopoulos, Panagiotis and Kyropoulou, Maria}, year={2025}, month=nov, pages={3257–3282} }
context:
Page 19, Section 7 (problems)

Also, in Section 5, we show how to compute iEF lotteries that maximize the expected log-Nash social welfare. Although maximizing log-Nash and average Nash social welfare are equivalent goals for deterministic allocations, this is not the case for lotteries. Computing iEF lotteries of maximum expected average Nash welfare is elusive at this point.
title: Compute interim envy-free lotteries maximizing expected average Nash welfare in matching instances
background_latex:
Consider a matching allocation instance with a set of agents \(N\) and a set of indivisible items \(I\) such that \(|N|=|I|=n\). Each agent \(i\in N\) has a nonnegative additive valuation \(v_i(S)=\sum_{g\in S} v_i(g)\) over bundles \(S\subseteq I\). A (deterministic) matching \(\mu\) assigns each agent \(i\) exactly one item \(\mu(i)\in I\), with each item assigned to exactly one agent.

A lottery \(Q\) is a probability distribution over matchings. If a matching \(\mu\sim Q\) is drawn, agent \(i\) receives the random item \(\mu(i)\). The lottery \(Q\) is interim envy-free (iEF) if for every ordered pair of agents \(i,j\in N\) and every item \(g\in I\) that agent \(i\) receives with positive probability under \(Q\) (i.e., \(\Pr_{\mu\sim Q}[\mu(i)=g]>0\)),
\[
v_i(g) \ge \mathbb{E}_{\mu\sim Q}\bigl[v_i(\mu(j))\mid \mu(i)=g\bigr].
\]

For a deterministic matching \(\mu\), the (average) Nash social welfare is the geometric mean of agents' utilities:
\[
\mathrm{avN}(\mu)=\Bigl(\prod_{i\in N} v_i(\mu(i))\Bigr)^{1/n}.
\]
For a lottery \(Q\), the objective of interest is the expected average Nash welfare \(\mathbb{E}_{\mu\sim Q}[\mathrm{avN}(\mu)]\).
problem_definition_latex:
Given a matching allocation instance \((N,I,(v_i)_{i\in N})\) with \(|N|=|I|\), determine how to compute an interim envy-free lottery \(Q\) that maximizes the expected average Nash social welfare
\[
\max_{Q\ \text{iEF}}\ \mathbb{E}_{\mu\sim Q}[\mathrm{avN}(\mu)].
\]
In particular, design an algorithm for this optimization problem (or determine its computational complexity).
latex:
Consider a matching allocation instance with a set of agents \(N\) and a set of indivisible items \(I\) such that \(|N|=|I|=n\). Each agent \(i\in N\) has a nonnegative additive valuation \(v_i(S)=\sum_{g\in S} v_i(g)\) over bundles \(S\subseteq I\). A (deterministic) matching \(\mu\) assigns each agent \(i\) exactly one item \(\mu(i)\in I\), with each item assigned to exactly one agent.

A lottery \(Q\) is a probability distribution over matchings. If a matching \(\mu\sim Q\) is drawn, agent \(i\) receives the random item \(\mu(i)\). The lottery \(Q\) is interim envy-free (iEF) if for every ordered pair of agents \(i,j\in N\) and every item \(g\in I\) that agent \(i\) receives with positive probability under \(Q\) (i.e., \(\Pr_{\mu\sim Q}[\mu(i)=g]>0\)),
\[
v_i(g) \ge \mathbb{E}_{\mu\sim Q}\bigl[v_i(\mu(j))\mid \mu(i)=g\bigr].
\]

For a deterministic matching \(\mu\), the (average) Nash social welfare is the geometric mean of agents' utilities:
\[
\mathrm{avN}(\mu)=\Bigl(\prod_{i\in N} v_i(\mu(i))\Bigr)^{1/n}.
\]
For a lottery \(Q\), the objective of interest is the expected average Nash welfare \(\mathbb{E}_{\mu\sim Q}[\mathrm{avN}(\mu)]\).

Given a matching allocation instance \((N,I,(v_i)_{i\in N})\) with \(|N|=|I|\), determine how to compute an interim envy-free lottery \(Q\) that maximizes the expected average Nash social welfare
\[
\max_{Q\ \text{iEF}}\ \mathbb{E}_{\mu\sim Q}[\mathrm{avN}(\mu)].
\]
In particular, design an algorithm for this optimization problem (or determine its computational complexity).
open_problem_quote: Also, in Section 5, we show how to compute iEF lotteries that maximize the expected log-Nash social welfare. Although maximizing log-Nash and average Nash social welfare are equivalent goals for deterministic allocations, this is not the case for lotteries. Computing iEF lotteries of maximum expected average Nash welfare is elusive at this point.
counterexample_suitability_rationale: Small matching instances can be exhaustively searched to compare the best iEF lottery under expected average Nash welfare versus other objectives, but the space of lotteries is continuous and typically requires solving nonconvex optimization, limiting pure instance-search approaches.
alphaevolve_suitability_rationale: Candidate algorithms (e.g., convex relaxations, iterative rounding, local search over supports of lotteries) can be represented programmatically and evaluated by computing iEF feasibility and numerically optimizing/estimating \(\mathbb{E}[\mathrm{avN}]\) on sampled or enumerated small instances, providing an automatic fitness signal for iterative improvement.

verification.notice: Generated from the paper text only and not independently verified as still open.

literature_review.synthesis:
The open problem originates in Caragiannis, Kanellopoulos, and Kyropoulou (EC 2021), which develops a linear-programming/ellipsoid framework for computing interim envy-free (iEF) lotteries in matching instances while optimizing several efficiency objectives. In particular, they show polynomial-time solvability for maximizing expected utilitarian welfare, expected egalitarian welfare, and expected log-Nash welfare by solving the dual LP with a separation oracle reducible to a novel combinatorial problem (maximum edge-pair-weighted perfect bipartite matching, 2EBM), itself solvable in polynomial time via Cruse’s decomposition theorem for centrosymmetric doubly stochastic matrices.

However, the paper explicitly notes that maximizing expected average Nash welfare \(\mathbb{E}[\mathrm{avN}(\mu)]\) under iEF is not captured by their log-Nash approach: while \(\mathrm{avN}(\mu)\) and \(\lgN(\mu)\) are equivalent objectives for deterministic matchings (monotone transforms), the equivalence breaks under expectation over lotteries. Thus the known polynomial-time algorithms do not resolve the stated objective, and no forward-citation evidence was provided indicating a later solution. Related literatures on random assignment (e.g., probabilistic serial) and deterministic maximum Nash welfare motivate the objective but do not address interim envy constraints or the nonlinearity of \(\mathbb{E}[\mathrm{avN}]\). At present, the most promising directions appear to be: (i) new convex/concave reformulations or approximation schemes for \(\mathbb{E}[\exp((1/n)\sum_i \ln v_i(\mu(i)))]\) under iEF constraints, (ii) complexity classification (e.g., NP-hardness via reductions exploiting the expectation-of-geometric-mean structure), or (iii) alternative algorithmic paradigms beyond ellipsoid+separation, potentially leveraging correlation gap/inequalities or restricting to structured valuation classes.

sources.title: On Interim Envy-Free Allocation Lotteries
sources.theorem_or_result: Introduces interim envy-freeness (iEF) for lotteries over allocations and gives polynomial-time algorithms (via LP duality + ellipsoid + a separation oracle based on a new 2-edge-pair-weighted bipartite matching problem) to compute iEF lotteries maximizing expected utilitarian welfare, egalitarian welfare, and expected log-Nash welfare in matching instances; explicitly leaves open maximizing expected average Nash welfare under iEF.
sources.connection: This is the source paper that formulates the open problem: while expected log-Nash welfare can be optimized under iEF in matching instances, optimizing expected average Nash welfare \(\mathbb{E}[\mathrm{avN}(\mu)]\) is stated as elusive because \(\mathbb{E}[\lgN(\mu)]\) and \(\mathbb{E}[\mathrm{avN}(\mu)]\) are no longer equivalent for lotteries.

sources.title: A New Solution to the Random Assignment Problem
sources.theorem_or_result: Introduces the probabilistic serial rule for random assignment, a canonical approach to computing fractional (ex-ante) fair and efficient random allocations in one-item-per-agent settings.
sources.connection: Provides foundational context for random assignment/matching with fairness constraints, though it targets ex-ante notions rather than interim envy-freeness and does not address maximizing expected Nash welfare under iEF.

sources.title: The Unreasonable Fairness of Maximum Nash Welfare
sources.theorem_or_result: Studies maximum Nash welfare (MNW) allocations for indivisible goods, establishing strong fairness/efficiency guarantees and algorithmic results for deterministic allocations.
sources.connection: Gives background on Nash welfare optimization in deterministic fair division; highlights why Nash-type objectives are central, but does not treat lotteries or interim envy-freeness.

sources.title: The Price of Fairness
sources.theorem_or_result: Introduces and analyzes the price of fairness framework quantifying efficiency loss due to fairness constraints.
sources.connection: The source paper analyzes the price of iEF for several welfare objectives; this paper is background for that efficiency-vs-fairness lens, not directly for the algorithmic open problem.

sources.title: On Bayesian Implementable Allocations
sources.theorem_or_result: Early work defining interim notions of incentive/fairness constraints in Bayesian settings; introduces interim envy-freeness terminology in a different context with fixed lotteries.
sources.connection: Historical origin of the iEF concept; provides conceptual grounding but not algorithmic tools for maximizing expected Nash welfare under iEF in matching instances.
